item.title: Active Style-Content Dual-Branch Domain Adaptation for Semi-Supervised SAR Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Xi Yang
Quantao Xie
Yirong Yang
Nannan Wang
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images offer unique advantages in all-weather, all-day remote sensing, but the high acquisition costs and time-consuming annotation processes limit their widespread implementation. Semi-supervised domain adaptation leverages abundant annotated optical images and a small number of labeled SAR images to achieve great performance on SAR images. However, existing semi-supervised domain adaptation object detection methods typically select SAR domain labeled samples randomly, making it difficult to fully exploit the valuable information and distinctive features inherent in the target domain data. Moreover, there is a significant style and content gap between optical and SAR images, and previous methods have not adapted to them in a task-specific manner. To this end, this paper proposes an active style-content dual-branch domain adaptation method specifically designed for semi-supervised object detection in SAR images. The proposed approach employs Task-aware Active Sampling (TAS) module to select the most valuable SAR samples, addressing inefficiencies in random sampling. Also, we employ a dual-branch framework to address the style and content gaps between optical and SAR images. Multi-layer Feature Alignment (MFA) module ensures style alignment by maintaining consistent feature representations across different visual styles, while Gaussian-SAM Image Fusion (G-SIF) module is employed to integrate content from the source domain into the target domain, effectively bridging the gap between optical and SAR images. Extensive experiments on multiple ship and aircraft datasets demonstrate the exceptional generalization capabilities of our proposed model. Published: 2026-03-27T19:53:28+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.880 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xi Yang; Quantao Xie; Yirong Yang; Nannan Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3675888

Relevance: 0.880 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images offer unique advantages in all-weather, all-day remote sensing, but the high acquisition costs and time-consuming annotation processes limit their widespread implementation. Semi-supervised domain adaptation leverages abundant annotated optical images and a small number of labeled SAR images to achieve great performance on SAR images. However, existing semi-supervised domain adaptation object detection methods typically select SAR domain labeled samples randomly, making it difficult to fully exploit the valuable information and distinctive features inherent in the target domain data. Moreover, there is a significant style and content gap between optical and SAR images, and previous methods have not adapted to them in a task-specific manner. To this end, this paper proposes an active style-content dual-branch domain adaptation method specifically designed for semi-supervised object detection in SAR images. The proposed approach employs Task-aware Active Sampling (TAS) module to select the most valuable SAR samples, addressing inefficiencies in random sampling. Also, we employ a dual-branch framework to address the style and content gaps between optical and SAR images. Multi-layer Feature Alignment (MFA) module ensures style alignment by maintaining consistent feature representations across different visual styles, while Gaussian-SAM Image Fusion (G-SIF) module is employed to integrate content from the source domain into the target domain, effectively bridging the gap between optical and SAR images. Extensive experiments on multiple ship and aircraft datasets demonstrate the exceptional generalization capabilities of our proposed model.

item.title: Prompt is All You Need: Prompting Foundation Models for Large-scale Self-supervised Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Jiaojiao Su
Qiwu Luo
Shuzhou Sun
Yuenan Hou
Xinyu Zhang
Janne Heikkilä
Chunhua Yang
Li Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: This paper addresses the important and challenging task of large-scale unsupervised semantic segmentation (LUSS). We present the first attempt to unleash the power of foundation models (FMs) for the challenging, dense prediction task LUSS, and our main objective is to present simple, effective yet efficient solutions for LUSS, namely Prompting foundation models for LUSS (PLUSS). Firstly, we proposed a cascade framework PLUSS _ α \_\alpha by effectively marrying CLIPS, Grounding DINO, and SAM in a zero-shot manner. This cascade architecture automatically generates semantic and spatial prompts for SAM, establishing a strong baseline that significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods. Building upon this foundation, we propose PLUSS _ β \_\beta , which addresses the critical bottleneck of prompt quality through two novel tuner modules: a semantic tuner that enhances fine-grained category discrimination via visual prompt tuning, and a box tuner that improves object localization through cross-modal feature fusion. Both tuners are optimized by capitalizing on the knowledge already present within the foundation models themselves, deriving self-supervised signals from internal model consistency. This approach requires no external supervision or updates to the foundation models' parameters. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S benchmarks demonstrate that PLUSS _ β \_\beta achieves remarkable performance improvements, surpassing the previous best method by 39.6%, 27.3%, and 22.6% in mIoU for 50, 300, and 919 categories respectively. Our approach exhibits robust category-shape representation across varying object sizes and dataset scales, while maintaining strong generalization capabilities for open-vocabulary tasks. The proposed framework provides a solid baseline for adapting foundation models to downstream vision tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/Miss-Jo/PLUSS_beta. Published: 2026-03-27T19:49:59+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.844 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiaojiao Su; Qiwu Luo; Shuzhou Sun; Yuenan Hou; Xinyu Zhang; Janne Heikkilä; Chunhua Yang; Li Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3673339

Relevance: 0.844 (must_read)

This paper addresses the important and challenging task of large-scale unsupervised semantic segmentation (LUSS). We present the first attempt to unleash the power of foundation models (FMs) for the challenging, dense prediction task LUSS, and our main objective is to present simple, effective yet efficient solutions for LUSS, namely Prompting foundation models for LUSS (PLUSS). Firstly, we proposed a cascade framework PLUSS _ α \_\alpha by effectively marrying CLIPS, Grounding DINO, and SAM in a zero-shot manner. This cascade architecture automatically generates semantic and spatial prompts for SAM, establishing a strong baseline that significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods. Building upon this foundation, we propose PLUSS _ β \_\beta , which addresses the critical bottleneck of prompt quality through two novel tuner modules: a semantic tuner that enhances fine-grained category discrimination via visual prompt tuning, and a box tuner that improves object localization through cross-modal feature fusion. Both tuners are optimized by capitalizing on the knowledge already present within the foundation models themselves, deriving self-supervised signals from internal model consistency. This approach requires no external supervision or updates to the foundation models' parameters. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S benchmarks demonstrate that PLUSS _ β \_\beta achieves remarkable performance improvements, surpassing the previous best method by 39.6%, 27.3%, and 22.6% in mIoU for 50, 300, and 919 categories respectively. Our approach exhibits robust category-shape representation across varying object sizes and dataset scales, while maintaining strong generalization capabilities for open-vocabulary tasks. The proposed framework provides a solid baseline for adapting foundation models to downstream vision tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/Miss-Jo/PLUSS_beta.

item.title: Azi-Vec: High-resolution Azimuth-sensitive PolSAR image generation for Vehicle automatic target recognition
item.dc:creator: Yuzhen Xie
Huaiyue Ding
Ye Liu
Nengcai Li
Deliang Xiang
Yi Su
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: High-resolution polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) has clear advantages for automatic target recognition (ATR) by capturing fine-grained scattering from ground objects. However, polarimetric responses vary significantly with the relative azimuth between the radar line of sight and the target. This sensitivity leads to multi-view feature inconsistency and feature confusion, which together degrade the accuracy and generalization of ATR models. We propose a multi-azimuth data generation method specifically designed for PolSAR vehicle targets. By using measured PolSAR data from two sampled azimuths as input, the method constructs a geometry-aware, azimuth-coupled sparse representation of polarimetric features. This representation serves as a prior to synthesize target images at arbitrary intermediate azimuths within the same vehicle class. Central to this approach is an azimuth-sensitive feature space that jointly models geometric edges, polarimetric cues, and imaging parameters. A physically constrained framework further enforces radar imaging geometry and scattering-structure consistency to simplify the learning of complex mappings. Experiments under standard operating conditions (SOC) and extended operating conditions (EOC) show that the generated images accurately reproduce key polarimetric scattering characteristics and geometric structures while adhering to PolSAR imaging principles. Compared with existing state-of-the-art methods including CVAE-GAN, ACGAN, ATGAN, and SD, our approach achieves superior performance, yielding an FID of 48.2844, a KID of 0.0135, an SSIM of 0.5058, and an FSIM of 0.7186. Vehicle ATR experiments further confirm the utility of the generated data. Replacing a portion of real training samples with synthetic ones results in accuracy decreases of only 0.55%, 0.94%, and 0.86% for VGG16, ResNet50, and a PolSAR recognition model, respectively. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/rainz-x/PolSAR-generation/tree/main . Published: 2026-03-28T08:31:07+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.827 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuzhen Xie; Huaiyue Ding; Ye Liu; Nengcai Li; Deliang Xiang; Yi Su

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.038

Relevance: 0.827 (must_read)

High-resolution polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) has clear advantages for automatic target recognition (ATR) by capturing fine-grained scattering from ground objects. However, polarimetric responses vary significantly with the relative azimuth between the radar line of sight and the target. This sensitivity leads to multi-view feature inconsistency and feature confusion, which together degrade the accuracy and generalization of ATR models. We propose a multi-azimuth data generation method specifically designed for PolSAR vehicle targets. By using measured PolSAR data from two sampled azimuths as input, the method constructs a geometry-aware, azimuth-coupled sparse representation of polarimetric features. This representation serves as a prior to synthesize target images at arbitrary intermediate azimuths within the same vehicle class. Central to this approach is an azimuth-sensitive feature space that jointly models geometric edges, polarimetric cues, and imaging parameters. A physically constrained framework further enforces radar imaging geometry and scattering-structure consistency to simplify the learning of complex mappings. Experiments under standard operating conditions (SOC) and extended operating conditions (EOC) show that the generated images accurately reproduce key polarimetric scattering characteristics and geometric structures while adhering to PolSAR imaging principles. Compared with existing state-of-the-art methods including CVAE-GAN, ACGAN, ATGAN, and SD, our approach achieves superior performance, yielding an FID of 48.2844, a KID of 0.0135, an SSIM of 0.5058, and an FSIM of 0.7186. Vehicle ATR experiments further confirm the utility of the generated data. Replacing a portion of real training samples with synthetic ones results in accuracy decreases of only 0.55%, 0.94%, and 0.86% for VGG16, ResNet50, and a PolSAR recognition model, respectively. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/rainz-x/PolSAR-generation/tree/main .

item.title: Harnessing the Power of Local Representations for Few-Shot Classification
item.dc:creator: Shi Tang
Guiming Luo
Xinchen Ye
Zhiyi Xia
item.description: Generalization to unseen classes is a central challenge in few-shot classification. While recent metric-based methods leverage local representations to address this, their effectiveness is often limited by two key issues: (i) inadequate supervision during feature extractor pretraining, and (ii) inflexible similarity metrics that struggle with diverse local feature set compositions. In this work, we fully harness the potential of local representations to overcome these limitations. First, we introduce a novel pretraining paradigm for the feature extractor that learns from randomly cropped image patches using soft labels. This approach captures class-level diversity while mitigating semantic misalignment from hard labels. To align network outputs with these soft labels, we propose the UniCon KL-Divergence loss, ensuring balanced contributions from all base classes for “non-base” patches. Second, for the similarity metric, we formulate the matching of local feature sets as an entropy-regularized optimal transport problem, effectively handling homogeneous sets. We further imbue the metric with adaptability through a dedicated Modulation Module. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three widely-used benchmarks, notably surpassing both transductive and cross-modal approaches in fine-grained classification scenarios. Published: 2026-03-28T07:26:26+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shi Tang; Guiming Luo; Xinchen Ye; Zhiyi Xia

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113597

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Generalization to unseen classes is a central challenge in few-shot classification. While recent metric-based methods leverage local representations to address this, their effectiveness is often limited by two key issues: (i) inadequate supervision during feature extractor pretraining, and (ii) inflexible similarity metrics that struggle with diverse local feature set compositions. In this work, we fully harness the potential of local representations to overcome these limitations. First, we introduce a novel pretraining paradigm for the feature extractor that learns from randomly cropped image patches using soft labels. This approach captures class-level diversity while mitigating semantic misalignment from hard labels. To align network outputs with these soft labels, we propose the UniCon KL-Divergence loss, ensuring balanced contributions from all base classes for “non-base” patches. Second, for the similarity metric, we formulate the matching of local feature sets as an entropy-regularized optimal transport problem, effectively handling homogeneous sets. We further imbue the metric with adaptability through a dedicated Modulation Module. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three widely-used benchmarks, notably surpassing both transductive and cross-modal approaches in fine-grained classification scenarios.

item.title: Frequency–Spatial Domain Jointly Guided Perceptual Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Yeteng Han
Minrui Ye
Bohan Liu
Jie Li
Chaoxian Jia
Wennan Cui
Tao Zhang
item.description: Infrared small target detection is a critical task in remote sensing. However, it remains highly challenging due to low contrast, heavy background clutter, and large variations in target scale. Traditional convolutional networks are inadequate for joint modeling, as they cannot effectively capture both fine structural details and global contextual dependencies. To address these issues, we propose FSGPNet, a frequency–spatial domain jointly guided perceptual network that explicitly exploits complementary representations in both the frequency and spatial domains. Specifically, a Frequency–Spatial Enhancement Module (FSEM) is introduced to strengthen target details while suppressing background interference through high-frequency enhancement and Perona–Malik diffusion. To enhance global context modeling, we propose a Multi-Scale Global Perception (MSGP) module that integrates non-local attention with multi-scale dilated convolutions, enabling robust background modeling. Furthermore, a Gabor Transformer Attention Module (GTAM) is designed to achieve selective frequency–spatial feature aggregation via self-attention over multi-directional and multi-scale Gabor responses, effectively highlighting discriminative structures of various small targets. Extensive experiments are conducted on two benchmark datasets (IRSTD-1K and NUDT-SIRST) that cover typical remote sensing infrared scenarios. Quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate that FSGPNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple evaluation metrics. These findings validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed FSGPNet for detecting small infrared targets in remote sensing applications. Published: 2026-03-27T11:32:47+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.805 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yeteng Han; Minrui Ye; Bohan Liu; Jie Li; Chaoxian Jia; Wennan Cui; Tao Zhang

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18071000

Relevance: 0.805 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection is a critical task in remote sensing. However, it remains highly challenging due to low contrast, heavy background clutter, and large variations in target scale. Traditional convolutional networks are inadequate for joint modeling, as they cannot effectively capture both fine structural details and global contextual dependencies. To address these issues, we propose FSGPNet, a frequency–spatial domain jointly guided perceptual network that explicitly exploits complementary representations in both the frequency and spatial domains. Specifically, a Frequency–Spatial Enhancement Module (FSEM) is introduced to strengthen target details while suppressing background interference through high-frequency enhancement and Perona–Malik diffusion. To enhance global context modeling, we propose a Multi-Scale Global Perception (MSGP) module that integrates non-local attention with multi-scale dilated convolutions, enabling robust background modeling. Furthermore, a Gabor Transformer Attention Module (GTAM) is designed to achieve selective frequency–spatial feature aggregation via self-attention over multi-directional and multi-scale Gabor responses, effectively highlighting discriminative structures of various small targets. Extensive experiments are conducted on two benchmark datasets (IRSTD-1K and NUDT-SIRST) that cover typical remote sensing infrared scenarios. Quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate that FSGPNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple evaluation metrics. These findings validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed FSGPNet for detecting small infrared targets in remote sensing applications.

item.title: Learning from the Waist: Aspect-Ratio-Aware Positive Sampling for Oriented Ship Detection
item.dc:creator: Dandan Ma
Hao Huang
Zhiyu Jiang
item.description: Ship detection in earth observation imagery has become a key research focus for maritime resource monitoring and environmental management applications. However, the random orientations, large aspect ratios, and substantial scale variations of ships pose significant challenges for accurate localization and classification. To tackle these challenges, we propose an aspect-ratio-aware one-stage oriented ship detection method with dynamic label assignment. Our approach incorporates three novel strategies: (1) Trigonometric Normalized Coder (TNC), which employs trigonometric functions with normalization to create a concise angle encoder-decoder that effectively resolves angular boundary discontinuity; (2) Ratio-Driven Dynamic Assignment (RDDA), which uniquely utilizes ship aspect ratios to adaptively adjust positive sample selection, particularly incorporating high-quality features from the waist of ship that conventional methods overlook, further enhanced by a two-dimensional Gaussian distribution to measure sample quality; and (3) Attention-Guided Feature Fusion Module (AGFFM), which strengthens multi-scale feature representation by applying spatial and channel attention mechanisms across feature pyramid levels while effectively fusing adjacent layers’ features. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (HRSC2016, HRSID, and SSDD+) demonstrate that our approach achieves superior localization and classification performance compared to state-of-the-art one-stage detectors, especially for ships with extreme aspect ratios and varying scales. Published: 2026-03-28T16:12:54+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dandan Ma; Hao Huang; Zhiyu Jiang

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132251

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Ship detection in earth observation imagery has become a key research focus for maritime resource monitoring and environmental management applications. However, the random orientations, large aspect ratios, and substantial scale variations of ships pose significant challenges for accurate localization and classification. To tackle these challenges, we propose an aspect-ratio-aware one-stage oriented ship detection method with dynamic label assignment. Our approach incorporates three novel strategies: (1) Trigonometric Normalized Coder (TNC), which employs trigonometric functions with normalization to create a concise angle encoder-decoder that effectively resolves angular boundary discontinuity; (2) Ratio-Driven Dynamic Assignment (RDDA), which uniquely utilizes ship aspect ratios to adaptively adjust positive sample selection, particularly incorporating high-quality features from the waist of ship that conventional methods overlook, further enhanced by a two-dimensional Gaussian distribution to measure sample quality; and (3) Attention-Guided Feature Fusion Module (AGFFM), which strengthens multi-scale feature representation by applying spatial and channel attention mechanisms across feature pyramid levels while effectively fusing adjacent layers’ features. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (HRSC2016, HRSID, and SSDD+) demonstrate that our approach achieves superior localization and classification performance compared to state-of-the-art one-stage detectors, especially for ships with extreme aspect ratios and varying scales.

item.title: LG–BiFusion: Local and Global Bidirectional LiDAR–Camera Fusion for 3D Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Rongyun Zhang
Yuxiang Xu
Peicheng Shi
Ping Xiao
Rongxiang Wang
Hongwei Ou
item.description: Recently, Three-dimensional (3D) object detection by fusing light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and camera input has become a new trend. However, most mainstream methods only perform a one-way fusion of LiDAR and camera data, and do not fully utilise the interdependent characteristics of the two modalities. Simultaneously, the fusion of global LiDAR voxel features and camera features often lacks fine–grained regional information, leading to suboptimal results. Therefore, a local–global bidirectional and complementary LiDAR–camera fusion 3D object detection model is proposed in this study. Specifically, the LiDAR and camera modalities are fused in both directions at the global level. Through the camera–enhanced LiDAR and LiDAR–enhanced camera modules, rich voxel features with strong semantic information and camera features with enhanced spatial awareness are obtained. Subsequently, the foreground points and the camera features are locally fused using the 3D thermal value response to obtain fine–grained local foreground features. Finally, the three improved features are dynamically selected for object detection by the adaptive feature aggregation module. Extensive experiments were conducted on the nuScenes and KITTI datasets. Compared with the classical multimodal fusion model BEVFusion, the LG–BiFusion proposed in this study achieved a 3.6% increase in mean average precision (mAP) and a 3.2% increase in NDS on the nuScenes dataset, and improved the mAP of the multimodal model EPNet++ in the automotive category by 2.01% on the KITTI dataset. These experiments proved the effectiveness of the proposed model. Published: 2026-03-28T16:12:30+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rongyun Zhang; Yuxiang Xu; Peicheng Shi; Ping Xiao; Rongxiang Wang; Hongwei Ou

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115827

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Recently, Three-dimensional (3D) object detection by fusing light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and camera input has become a new trend. However, most mainstream methods only perform a one-way fusion of LiDAR and camera data, and do not fully utilise the interdependent characteristics of the two modalities. Simultaneously, the fusion of global LiDAR voxel features and camera features often lacks fine–grained regional information, leading to suboptimal results. Therefore, a local–global bidirectional and complementary LiDAR–camera fusion 3D object detection model is proposed in this study. Specifically, the LiDAR and camera modalities are fused in both directions at the global level. Through the camera–enhanced LiDAR and LiDAR–enhanced camera modules, rich voxel features with strong semantic information and camera features with enhanced spatial awareness are obtained. Subsequently, the foreground points and the camera features are locally fused using the 3D thermal value response to obtain fine–grained local foreground features. Finally, the three improved features are dynamically selected for object detection by the adaptive feature aggregation module. Extensive experiments were conducted on the nuScenes and KITTI datasets. Compared with the classical multimodal fusion model BEVFusion, the LG–BiFusion proposed in this study achieved a 3.6% increase in mean average precision (mAP) and a 3.2% increase in NDS on the nuScenes dataset, and improved the mAP of the multimodal model EPNet++ in the automotive category by 2.01% on the KITTI dataset. These experiments proved the effectiveness of the proposed model.

item.title: EarthMapper: Visual Autoregressive Models for Controllable Bidirectional Satellite–Map Translation
item.dc:creator: Yuzhe Sun
Zhe Dong
Haochen Jiang
Tianzhu Liu
Yanfeng Gu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Cross-modal translation between satellite imagery and cartographic maps constitutes a foundational challenge in geospatial artificial intelligence, with critical applications ranging from autonomous mapping systems to disaster response infrastructures. While existing methods attempt to address unidirectional satellite-to-map translation, the bidirectional satellite-map translation (BSMT) problem remains formally undefined and technically under-explored, particularly due to: (i) non-differentiable geometric discrepancies between pixel-level observations and symbolic representations, causing cross-modal feature misalignment; (ii) conflicting learning objectives requiring simultaneous high-level geographic abstraction and pixel-wise photorealism. To address these limitations, we introduce EarthMapper, a novel autoregressive framework for controllable bidirectional satellite-map translation. EarthMapper employs geographic coordinate embeddings to anchor generation, ensuring region-specific adaptability, and leverages multi-scale feature alignment within a geo-conditioned joint scale autoregression (GJSA) process to unify bidirectional translation in a single training cycle. A semantic infusion (SI) mechanism is introduced to enhance feature-level consistency, while a key point adaptive guidance (KPAG) mechanism is proposed to dynamically balance diversity and precision during inference. We further contribute CNSatMap, a large-scale dataset comprising 302,132 precisely aligned satellite-map pairs across 38 Chinese cities, enabling robust benchmarking. Extensive experiments on CNSatMap and the New York dataset demonstrate EarthMapper’s superior performance, achieving significant improvements in visual realism, semantic consistency, and structural fidelity over state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, EarthMapper excels in zero-shot tasks like in-painting, out-painting, and coordinate-conditional generation, underscoring its versatility. The source code for EarthMapper and ... Published: 2026-03-27T19:49:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.793 (must_read)

While existing methods attempt to address unidirectional satellite-to-map translation, the bidirectional satellite-map translation (BSMT) problem remains formally undefined and technically under-explored, particularly due to: (i) non-differentiable geometric discrepancies between pixel-level observations and symbolic representations, causing cross-modal feature misalignment; (ii) conflicting learning objectives requiring simultaneous high-level geographic abstraction and pixel-wise photorealism. To address these limitations, we introduce EarthMapper, a novel autoregressive framework for controllable bidirectional satellite-map translation. EarthMapper employs geographic coordinate embeddings to anchor generation, ensuring region-specific adaptability, and leverages multi-scale feature alignment within a geo-conditioned joint scale autoregression (GJSA) process to unify bidirectional translation in a single training cycle. A semantic infusion (SI) mechanism is introduced to enhance feature-level consistency, while a key point adaptive guidance (KPAG) mechanism is proposed to dynamically balance diversity and precision during inference. We further contribute CNSatMap, a large-scale dataset comprising 302,132 precisely aligned satellite-map pairs across 38 Chinese cities, enabling robust benchmarking. Extensive experiments on CNSatMap and the New York dataset demonstrate EarthMapper’s superior performance, achieving significant improvements in visual realism, semantic consistency, and structural fidelity over state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, EarthMapper excels in zero-shot tasks like in-painting, out-painting, and coordinate-conditional generation, underscoring its versatility. The source code for EarthMapper and ...

item.title: Context-Enhanced and Scale-Contrastive Learning for Remote Sensing Imagery Understanding
item.dc:creator: Aiping Hu
Rongting Zhang
Guangyun Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown strong potential for learning representations from unlabeled data, yet its direct application to remote sensing (RS) imagery remains challenging due to complex backgrounds and severe target-scale variations. To address these challenges, we propose Context-Enhanced Semantic Contrast (CESC), a self-supervised pre-training framework tailored for RS imagery. CESC integrates masked reconstruction, explicit contextual enhancement, and cross-scale semantic contrast to jointly model fine-grained spatial structures and scale-invariant semantic representations. Extensive experiments on scene classification and semantic segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that CESC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SSL methods under equivalent settings, validating its effectiveness and transferability for RS representation learning. Published: 2026-03-27T19:53:59+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Aiping Hu; Rongting Zhang; Guangyun Zhang

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3678388

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown strong potential for learning representations from unlabeled data, yet its direct application to remote sensing (RS) imagery remains challenging due to complex backgrounds and severe target-scale variations. To address these challenges, we propose Context-Enhanced Semantic Contrast (CESC), a self-supervised pre-training framework tailored for RS imagery. CESC integrates masked reconstruction, explicit contextual enhancement, and cross-scale semantic contrast to jointly model fine-grained spatial structures and scale-invariant semantic representations. Extensive experiments on scene classification and semantic segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that CESC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SSL methods under equivalent settings, validating its effectiveness and transferability for RS representation learning.

item.title: Beyond Signal-to-Clutter Ratio: A Multi-Factor Detectability Framework for Infrared Dim Small Targets
item.dc:creator: Erwei Zhao
Bo Liu
Zixu Huang
Yinan Xing
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Detecting infrared dim small targets remains a significant challenge due to their extremely low signal strength submerged in complex background clutter and noise. Existing evaluation metrics, primarily the Signal-to-Clutter Ratio (SCR), treat backgrounds merely as statistical noise, failing to capture structural textures or target-like interferences. This inadequacy hinders the robust assessment and development of detection algorithms. To address this limitation, we propose a novel multi-factor detectability framework that goes beyond simple contrast measures. Our approach systematically decomposes and quantifies detection difficulty through three key dimensions: target salience, background complexity, and target confusion. By integrating these quantifiable factors, we derive a comprehensive and computable Target Detectability (TD) metric. Extensive experiments conducted on diverse real-world datasets demonstrate that our TD metric aligns significantly better with actual detection challenges than traditional indicators. Specifically, the proposed metric exhibits a much stronger correlation with the detection performance (AUC) of popular algorithms compared to SCR, effectively revealing difficulty variations that SCR overlooks. This framework can provide a more accurate and robust standard for assessing data quality and task difficulty, offering critical guidance for objective benchmarking and the targeted optimization of detection algorithms in sophisticated infrared surveillance scenarios. Published: 2026-03-27T19:50:21+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Erwei Zhao; Bo Liu; Zixu Huang; Yinan Xing

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3678394

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Detecting infrared dim small targets remains a significant challenge due to their extremely low signal strength submerged in complex background clutter and noise. Existing evaluation metrics, primarily the Signal-to-Clutter Ratio (SCR), treat backgrounds merely as statistical noise, failing to capture structural textures or target-like interferences. This inadequacy hinders the robust assessment and development of detection algorithms. To address this limitation, we propose a novel multi-factor detectability framework that goes beyond simple contrast measures. Our approach systematically decomposes and quantifies detection difficulty through three key dimensions: target salience, background complexity, and target confusion. By integrating these quantifiable factors, we derive a comprehensive and computable Target Detectability (TD) metric. Extensive experiments conducted on diverse real-world datasets demonstrate that our TD metric aligns significantly better with actual detection challenges than traditional indicators. Specifically, the proposed metric exhibits a much stronger correlation with the detection performance (AUC) of popular algorithms compared to SCR, effectively revealing difficulty variations that SCR overlooks. This framework can provide a more accurate and robust standard for assessing data quality and task difficulty, offering critical guidance for objective benchmarking and the targeted optimization of detection algorithms in sophisticated infrared surveillance scenarios.

item.title: Fre-STformer: A Frequency-based Spatio-Temporal Transformer for UAV Human Action Recognition
item.dc:creator: Tianqi Xiang
Xinghui Xia
Junsong Yuan
Zhigang Tu
item.description: Recent advances in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have expanded their applications in human action recognition. However, existing methods struggle with background interference from wide field-of-view and UAV-induced jitter. In this work, we propose the single stage Frequency-SpatioTemporal Transformer (Fre-STformer) model, which accurately recognizes human actions via mining feature variation patterns in both actions and backgrounds. Specifically, the proposed frequency based spatio-temporal (Fre-ST) module addresses two key challenges in UAV-based action recognition: spatial-frequency joint encoding suppresses background interference through spectral feature decomposition, while channel-level difference modeling stabilizes motion representations against UAV jitter via channel wise correlation learning. Finally, by incorporating the Fre-ST module, our Fre-STformer parses global spatio-temporal action features end-to-end more effectively, avoiding additional preprocessing, e.g. action region segmentation. Extensive experiments conducted on three typical low-altitude UAV datasets, i.e. UAV Human, NEC Drone and UAV-human-night. Results show that our Fre-STformer outperforms the state-of-the-arts on the first two datasets by 3.5% and 3.8% in Top-1 accuracy, respectively. In addition, on the third UAV-human-night dataset, the Top-1 accuracy boosts by at least 1.0%, validating the generalization of our Fre-STformer in low-light environments. Published: 2026-03-27T19:51:53+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.787 (must_read)

Recent advances in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have expanded their applications in human action recognition. However, existing methods struggle with background interference from wide field-of-view and UAV-induced jitter. In this work, we propose the single stage Frequency-SpatioTemporal Transformer (Fre-STformer) model, which accurately recognizes human actions via mining feature variation patterns in both actions and backgrounds. Specifically, the proposed frequency based spatio-temporal (Fre-ST) module addresses two key challenges in UAV-based action recognition: spatial-frequency joint encoding suppresses background interference through spectral feature decomposition, while channel-level difference modeling stabilizes motion representations against UAV jitter via channel wise correlation learning. Finally, by incorporating the Fre-ST module, our Fre-STformer parses global spatio-temporal action features end-to-end more effectively, avoiding additional preprocessing, e.g. action region segmentation. Extensive experiments conducted on three typical low-altitude UAV datasets, i.e. UAV Human, NEC Drone and UAV-human-night. Results show that our Fre-STformer outperforms the state-of-the-arts on the first two datasets by 3.5% and 3.8% in Top-1 accuracy, respectively. In addition, on the third UAV-human-night dataset, the Top-1 accuracy boosts by at least 1.0%, validating the generalization of our Fre-STformer in low-light environments.

item.title: SG-YOLO: A Multispectral Small-Object Detector for UAV Imagery Based on YOLO
item.dc:creator: Binjie Zhang
Lin Wang
Quanwei Yao
Keyang Li
Qinyan Tan
item.description: Object detection in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery remains a crucial yet challenging task due to complex backgrounds, large scale variations, and the prevalence of small objects. Visible-spectrum images lack robustness under all-weather and all-illumination conditions; by contrast, multispectral sensing provides complementary cues (e.g., thermal signatures) that improve detection robustness. However, existing multispectral solutions often incur high computational costs and are therefore difficult to deploy on resource-constrained UAV platforms. To address these issues, SG-YOLO is proposed, a lightweight and efficient multispectral object detection framework that aims to balance accuracy and efficiency. First, a Spectral Gated Downsampling Stem (SGDS) is designed, in which grouped convolutions and a gating mechanism are employed at the early stage of the network to extract band-specific features, thereby maximizing spectral complementarity while minimizing redundancy. Second, a Spectral–Spatial Iterative Attention Fusion (SSIAF) module is introduced, in which spectral-wise (channel) attention and spatial-wise attention are iteratively coupled and cascaded in a multi-scale manner to jointly model cross-band dependencies and spatial saliency, thereby aggregating high-level semantic information while suppressing redundant spectral responses. Finally, a Spatial–Channel Synergistic Fusion (SCSF) module is designed to enhance multi-scale and cross-channel feature integration in the neck. Experiments on the MODA dataset show that SG-YOLOs achieves 72.4% mAP50, outperforming the baseline by 3.2%. Moreover, compared with a range of mainstream one-stage detectors and multispectral detection methods, SG-YOLO delivers the best overall performance, providing an effective solution for UAV object detection while maintaining a favorable trade-off between model size and detection accuracy. Published: 2026-03-27T14:33:33+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.784 (consider)

Authors: Binjie Zhang; Lin Wang; Quanwei Yao; Keyang Li; Qinyan Tan

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18071003

Relevance: 0.784 (consider)

Object detection in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery remains a crucial yet challenging task due to complex backgrounds, large scale variations, and the prevalence of small objects. Visible-spectrum images lack robustness under all-weather and all-illumination conditions; by contrast, multispectral sensing provides complementary cues (e.g., thermal signatures) that improve detection robustness. However, existing multispectral solutions often incur high computational costs and are therefore difficult to deploy on resource-constrained UAV platforms. To address these issues, SG-YOLO is proposed, a lightweight and efficient multispectral object detection framework that aims to balance accuracy and efficiency. First, a Spectral Gated Downsampling Stem (SGDS) is designed, in which grouped convolutions and a gating mechanism are employed at the early stage of the network to extract band-specific features, thereby maximizing spectral complementarity while minimizing redundancy. Second, a Spectral–Spatial Iterative Attention Fusion (SSIAF) module is introduced, in which spectral-wise (channel) attention and spatial-wise attention are iteratively coupled and cascaded in a multi-scale manner to jointly model cross-band dependencies and spatial saliency, thereby aggregating high-level semantic information while suppressing redundant spectral responses. Finally, a Spatial–Channel Synergistic Fusion (SCSF) module is designed to enhance multi-scale and cross-channel feature integration in the neck. Experiments on the MODA dataset show that SG-YOLOs achieves 72.4% mAP50, outperforming the baseline by 3.2%. Moreover, compared with a range of mainstream one-stage detectors and multispectral detection methods, SG-YOLO delivers the best overall performance, providing an effective solution for UAV object detection while maintaining a favorable trade-off between model size and detection accuracy.

item.title: MapSAM2: Adapting SAM2 for Automatic Segmentation of Historical Map Images and Time Series
item.dc:creator: Xue Xia
Randall Balestriero
Tao Zhang
Yixin Zhou
Andrew Ding
Dev Saini
Lorenz Hurni
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Historical maps are unique and valuable archives that document geographic features across different time periods. However, automated analysis of historical map images remains a significant challenge due to their wide stylistic variability and the scarcity of annotated training data. Constructing linked spatio-temporal datasets from historical map time series is even more time-consuming and labor-intensive, as it requires synthesizing information from multiple maps. Such datasets are essential for applications such as dating buildings, analyzing the development of road networks and settlements, studying environmental changes etc. We present MapSAM2, a unified framework for automatically segmenting both historical map images and time series. Built on a visual foundation model, MapSAM2 adapts to diverse segmentation tasks with few-shot fine-tuning. Our key innovation is to treat both historical map images and time series as videos. For images, we process a set of tiles as a video, enabling the memory attention mechanism to incorporate contextual cues from similar tiles, leading to improved geometric accuracy, particularly for areal features. For time series, we introduce the annotated Siegfried Building Time Series Dataset and, to reduce annotation costs, propose generating pseudo time series from single-year maps by simulating common temporal transformations. Experimental results show that MapSAM2 learns temporal associations effectively and can accurately segment and link buildings in time series under limited supervision or using pseudo videos. We release both our dataset and code to support future research. Published: 2026-03-27T19:49:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xue Xia; Randall Balestriero; Tao Zhang; Yixin Zhou; Andrew Ding; Dev Saini; Lorenz Hurni

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3678420

Relevance: 0.782 (consider)

Historical maps are unique and valuable archives that document geographic features across different time periods. However, automated analysis of historical map images remains a significant challenge due to their wide stylistic variability and the scarcity of annotated training data. Constructing linked spatio-temporal datasets from historical map time series is even more time-consuming and labor-intensive, as it requires synthesizing information from multiple maps. Such datasets are essential for applications such as dating buildings, analyzing the development of road networks and settlements, studying environmental changes etc. We present MapSAM2, a unified framework for automatically segmenting both historical map images and time series. Built on a visual foundation model, MapSAM2 adapts to diverse segmentation tasks with few-shot fine-tuning. Our key innovation is to treat both historical map images and time series as videos. For images, we process a set of tiles as a video, enabling the memory attention mechanism to incorporate contextual cues from similar tiles, leading to improved geometric accuracy, particularly for areal features. For time series, we introduce the annotated Siegfried Building Time Series Dataset and, to reduce annotation costs, propose generating pseudo time series from single-year maps by simulating common temporal transformations. Experimental results show that MapSAM2 learns temporal associations effectively and can accurately segment and link buildings in time series under limited supervision or using pseudo videos. We release both our dataset and code to support future research.

item.title: Towards Efficient Language Giants: A Comprehensive Survey on Structural Optimizations and Compression Techniques for Large Language Models
item.dc:creator: Gilhyeon Lee
Seonggeun Kim
Dongjun Lee
Kyungmin Goh
Hyun Kim
item.description: The impressive success of large language models (LLMs) across a broad spectrum of NLP tasks has attracted considerable attention in both academia and industry. However, their inference incurs substantial computational and memory overhead, making it challenging to efficiently deploy them in latency-sensitive or resource-constrained environments. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of model-level optimization techniques developed to address these issues. Specifically, we examine efficient module designs for core Transformer components–including multi-head attention, feed-forward networks, normalization, and positional encoding–and summarize representative compression methods such as quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, approximation, and parameter sharing. We categorize recent advances in each area, analyzing their algorithmic principles, empirical trade-offs, and impact on inference efficiency. Furthermore, we propose practical design strategies that effectively combine efficient modules with compression techniques to significantly reduce inference costs while meeting application-specific accuracy and latency requirements. Published: 2026-03-28T16:11:01+00:00 Venue: Neural Networks Score: 0.781 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Gilhyeon Lee; Seonggeun Kim; Dongjun Lee; Kyungmin Goh; Hyun Kim

Journal: Neural Networks

DOI: 10.1016/j.neunet.2026.108900

Relevance: 0.781 (consider)

The impressive success of large language models (LLMs) across a broad spectrum of NLP tasks has attracted considerable attention in both academia and industry. However, their inference incurs substantial computational and memory overhead, making it challenging to efficiently deploy them in latency-sensitive or resource-constrained environments. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of model-level optimization techniques developed to address these issues. Specifically, we examine efficient module designs for core Transformer components–including multi-head attention, feed-forward networks, normalization, and positional encoding–and summarize representative compression methods such as quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, approximation, and parameter sharing. We categorize recent advances in each area, analyzing their algorithmic principles, empirical trade-offs, and impact on inference efficiency. Furthermore, we propose practical design strategies that effectively combine efficient modules with compression techniques to significantly reduce inference costs while meeting application-specific accuracy and latency requirements.

item.title: CLIP-Enhanced Contrastive Clustering for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Yan Zeng
Yun Ge
Jinlong Xu
Yuanyuan Shen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: With the rapid growth of unlabeled remote sensing images, supervised learning methods relying on manual annotation have difficulty to meet the demands of large-scale data. Self-supervised contrastive learning provides an effective mechanism to leverage extensive unannotated datasets. However, these methods mainly focus on instance-level discrimination and ignore the underlying clustering distribution. To address this problem, we propose CLIP-Enhanced Contrastive Clustering (CCC), a triple-branch framework designed for remote sensing image retrieval that utilizes the underlying clustering distribution to guide contrastive learning. Specially, CCC proposes a CLIP-based textual branch that constructs auxiliary cluster distributions using only category number, thereby forming a hybrid framework that couples visual contrastive clustering with text-derived semantic priors, eliminating the need for textual descriptions of remote sensing images. By designing a KL-divergence cluster center loss, we enhance intra-cluster compactness through text-guided visual cluster alignment. To better leverage the learning ability of the model, we also design a learning attention allocation strategy to shift focus from instance-level discrimination to cluster-level consistency. Experimental results show that CCC significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of mAP, ANMRR, and P@k metrics, greatly reducing the reliance on costly manual annotations while maintaining robustness against inconsistent pseudo-labeling. Our code is made public at https://github.com/zeng1011/CCC. Published: 2026-03-27T19:50:21+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.780 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

With the rapid growth of unlabeled remote sensing images, supervised learning methods relying on manual annotation have difficulty to meet the demands of large-scale data. Self-supervised contrastive learning provides an effective mechanism to leverage extensive unannotated datasets. However, these methods mainly focus on instance-level discrimination and ignore the underlying clustering distribution. To address this problem, we propose CLIP-Enhanced Contrastive Clustering (CCC), a triple-branch framework designed for remote sensing image retrieval that utilizes the underlying clustering distribution to guide contrastive learning. Specially, CCC proposes a CLIP-based textual branch that constructs auxiliary cluster distributions using only category number, thereby forming a hybrid framework that couples visual contrastive clustering with text-derived semantic priors, eliminating the need for textual descriptions of remote sensing images. By designing a KL-divergence cluster center loss, we enhance intra-cluster compactness through text-guided visual cluster alignment. To better leverage the learning ability of the model, we also design a learning attention allocation strategy to shift focus from instance-level discrimination to cluster-level consistency. Experimental results show that CCC significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of mAP, ANMRR, and P@k metrics, greatly reducing the reliance on costly manual annotations while maintaining robustness against inconsistent pseudo-labeling. Our code is made public at https://github.com/zeng1011/CCC.

item.title: A continual evolving Visual-Inertial Odometry for drones in flight
item.dc:creator: Yangfan Zhou
Wenhui Wei
Renshu Li
Xin Liu
Jiadong Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Deep learning-based Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) algorithms for drones show great application potential, but their model parameters are typically fixed and cannot be optimized online during flight. These fixed models lack the ability to self-evolve to changes in drone flight scenarios, leading to significant fluctuations in positioning accuracy. To address this limitation, we propose Evolvable VIO (E-VIO), a novel approach that enables real-time online training and inference on flying drones. Our approach leverages a robust dual-replay-memory continual learning strategy to facilitate efficient online adaptation, while a minimalist Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-Transformer cascade backbone ensures low computational complexity. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that under changing environmental conditions, E-VIO improves the average positioning accuracy by 84.60% compared to fixed-model VIO. Furthermore, it maintains real-time performance with an inference speed of 21.2 fps and an online training speed of 5.0 fps on an edge computing platform. To benefit the community, we make the source code publicly available. Published: 2026-03-28T05:25:43+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.778 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yangfan Zhou; Wenhui Wei; Renshu Li; Xin Liu; Jiadong Li

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.039

Relevance: 0.778 (consider)

Deep learning-based Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) algorithms for drones show great application potential, but their model parameters are typically fixed and cannot be optimized online during flight. These fixed models lack the ability to self-evolve to changes in drone flight scenarios, leading to significant fluctuations in positioning accuracy. To address this limitation, we propose Evolvable VIO (E-VIO), a novel approach that enables real-time online training and inference on flying drones. Our approach leverages a robust dual-replay-memory continual learning strategy to facilitate efficient online adaptation, while a minimalist Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-Transformer cascade backbone ensures low computational complexity. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that under changing environmental conditions, E-VIO improves the average positioning accuracy by 84.60% compared to fixed-model VIO. Furthermore, it maintains real-time performance with an inference speed of 21.2 fps and an online training speed of 5.0 fps on an edge computing platform. To benefit the community, we make the source code publicly available.

item.title: Seg-LLaVA: Empowering Pixel-Level Understanding with Large Vision Language Model
item.dc:creator: Fan Yang
Yousong Zhu
Yufei Zhan
Hongyin Zhao
Xin Li
Yaowei Wang
Ming Tang
Xin Ning
Jinqiao Wang
item.description: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) demonstrate strong performance on high-level semantic tasks but lack fine-grained perception for pixel-level understanding, particularly in Referring Expression Segmentation (RES). The core challenge is representing irregular object contours compatibly with the sequential nature of language models. We introduce Seg-LLaVA, an end-to-end LVLM that redefines segmentation by predicting contour points in polar coordinates rather than dense pixel masks. Our Polar Coordinate Adaptive Sampling (PCAS) samples key boundary points, providing a unified representation that enhances training stability and shape fidelity. A lightweight refinement module leverages hidden states to generate high-precision masks with improved boundaries. We also introduce LAGS, a large-scale dataset enabling complex, interactive, and multi-object video segmentation. Extensive experiments show Seg-LLaVA achieves state-of-the-art performance, substantially surpassing previous methods in segmentation accuracy, localization precision, and language grounding. Published: 2026-03-27T16:49:32+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.775 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fan Yang; Yousong Zhu; Yufei Zhan; Hongyin Zhao; Xin Li; Yaowei Wang; Ming Tang; Xin Ning; Jinqiao Wang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113560

Relevance: 0.775 (consider)

Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) demonstrate strong performance on high-level semantic tasks but lack fine-grained perception for pixel-level understanding, particularly in Referring Expression Segmentation (RES). The core challenge is representing irregular object contours compatibly with the sequential nature of language models. We introduce Seg-LLaVA, an end-to-end LVLM that redefines segmentation by predicting contour points in polar coordinates rather than dense pixel masks. Our Polar Coordinate Adaptive Sampling (PCAS) samples key boundary points, providing a unified representation that enhances training stability and shape fidelity. A lightweight refinement module leverages hidden states to generate high-precision masks with improved boundaries. We also introduce LAGS, a large-scale dataset enabling complex, interactive, and multi-object video segmentation. Extensive experiments show Seg-LLaVA achieves state-of-the-art performance, substantially surpassing previous methods in segmentation accuracy, localization precision, and language grounding.

item.title: DSCD: A Dual-Stream Cross-Domain Network for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
item.dc:creator: Yulin Cai
Zhipeng Chang
Lukang Wang
Xinglu Wang
Wenlu Liu
Yue Zhuo
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: The domain adaptation method for cross-domain classification alleviates the dependence of deep learning on a large amount of labeled data in hyperspectral image classification (HSI), but it still requires a relatively adequate number of labeled samples from the source domain to achieve stable transfer effects. Meanwhile, cross-domain few-shot classification is constrained by distribution discrepancies and category mismatches between source and target domains. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Dual-Stream Cross-Domain Network (DSCD) based on existing few-shot networks, aiming to explore how to effectively transfer source domain knowledge to improve learning efficiency in the target domain under limited annotations. Within a meta-learning paradigm, DSCD integrates feature discriminability and domain adaptability through a twostage collaborative optimization mechanism to improve crossdomain few-shot classification performance. In the first stage, a Spectral-Spatial Weighting Module (SSWM) is designed by combining a spectral-spatial joint attention mechanism with depthwise separable convolutions, which enhances feature discriminability while simplifying the model structure. In the second stage, a Feature Embedding Module (FEM) is developed to leverage the complementary strengths of 2D and 3D convolutions. Utilizing Octave Convolution, FEM facilitates a dual-path interaction between low- and high-frequency components, along with the inter-frequency feature exchange mechanism, enables deep mining of spectral-spatial representations. Experiments on four benchmark datasets- Pavia Center (PC), Pavia University (PU), Salinas (SA), and Indian Pines (IP)-demonstrate that DSCD achieves overall classification accuracies of 97.49%, 82.59%, 92.02%, and 77.89%, respectively. Furthermore, all experiments were conducted under a cross-domain 1-shot few-shot setting, where only one labeled sample per category in the target domain was provided for model adaptation. These r... Meanwhile, cross-domain few-shot classification is constrained by distribution discrepancies and category mismatches between source and target domains. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Dual-Stream Cross-Domain Network (DSCD) based on existing few-shot networks, aiming to explore how to effectively transfer source domain knowledge to improve learning efficiency in the target domain under limited annotations. Within a meta-learning paradigm, DSCD integrates feature discriminability and domain adaptability through a twostage collaborative optimization mechanism to improve crossdomain few-shot classification performance. In the first stage, a Spectral-Spatial Weighting Module (SSWM) is designed by combining a spectral-spatial joint attention mechanism with depthwise separable convolutions, which enhances feature discriminability while simplifying the model structure. In the second stage, a Feature Embedding Module (FEM) is developed to leverage the complementary strengths of 2D and 3D convolutions. Utilizing Octave Convolution, FEM facilitates a dual-path interaction between low- and high-frequency components, along with the inter-frequency feature exchange mechanism, enables deep mining of spectral-spatial representations. Experiments on four benchmark datasets- Pavia Center (PC), Pavia University (PU), Salinas (SA), and Indian Pines (IP)-demonstrate that DSCD achieves overall classification accuracies of 97.49%, 82.59%, 92.02%, and 77.89%, respectively. Furthermore, all experiments were conducted under a cross-domain 1-shot few-shot setting, where only one labeled sample per category in the target domain was provided for model adaptation. item.title: Dynamic Expansion Orthogonal Network for Class-incremental Learning
item.dc:creator: Mingda Dong
Zhizhong Zhang
Xin Tan
Jiling Qiu
Yuan Xie
item.description: Dynamic expansion networks have emerged as a promising approach for incremental learning by introducing new feature extractors for each task while freezing previously learned ones to preserve acquired knowledge. However, existing methods often fail to effectively leverage information from earlier tasks when learning subsequent tasks, leading to blurred decision boundaries for earlier tasks and exacerbating catastrophic forgetting. To address this issue, we propose a novel orthogonal feature space constraint that encourages new-task features to lie in a subspace orthogonal to that of earlier tasks. This constraint helps preserves task boundaries and mitigate catastrophic forgetting. However, strictly enforcing orthogonality may undermine the plasticity needed to learn new tasks, potentially hindering performance. To strike a balance between stability and plasticity, we introduce a selective orthogonal constraint guided by a Task Relevance Matrix (TRM), which quantifies cross-class similarities between old and new classes, relaxes unnecessary restrictions, and enables more precise and adaptive orthogonal regularization. Additionally, we propose an intra-task orthogonality module that helps new tasks better differentiate their feature space from that of old tasks. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, and ImageNet demonstrate that our method effectively alleviates catastrophic forgetting while maintaining competitive performance on newly introduced tasks. Published: 2026-03-27T08:15:31+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.774 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mingda Dong; Zhizhong Zhang; Xin Tan; Jiling Qiu; Yuan Xie

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115868

Relevance: 0.774 (consider)

Dynamic expansion networks have emerged as a promising approach for incremental learning by introducing new feature extractors for each task while freezing previously learned ones to preserve acquired knowledge. However, existing methods often fail to effectively leverage information from earlier tasks when learning subsequent tasks, leading to blurred decision boundaries for earlier tasks and exacerbating catastrophic forgetting. To address this issue, we propose a novel orthogonal feature space constraint that encourages new-task features to lie in a subspace orthogonal to that of earlier tasks. This constraint helps preserves task boundaries and mitigate catastrophic forgetting. However, strictly enforcing orthogonality may undermine the plasticity needed to learn new tasks, potentially hindering performance. To strike a balance between stability and plasticity, we introduce a selective orthogonal constraint guided by a Task Relevance Matrix (TRM), which quantifies cross-class similarities between old and new classes, relaxes unnecessary restrictions, and enables more precise and adaptive orthogonal regularization. Additionally, we propose an intra-task orthogonality module that helps new tasks better differentiate their feature space from that of old tasks. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, and ImageNet demonstrate that our method effectively alleviates catastrophic forgetting while maintaining competitive performance on newly introduced tasks.

item.title: PTCNet: Pure Transformer Network for Two-View Correspondence Pruning
item.dc:creator: Xiangyang Miao
Shunxing Chen
Shiping Wang
Junwen Guo
Fengying Wu
Guobao Xiao
Zongjuan Li
item.description: In this paper, we introduce a novel pure Transformer-based two-view correspondence pruning network (called PTCNet). Unlike existing methods that rely on Multilayer Perceptrons, PTCNet leverages a Transformer backbone to achieve superior performance and higher efficiency. Specifically, we propose the Multi-Perspective Blending Module (MPBM), which integrates two distinct Transformer-based blocks: the Dual-Aggregation Transformer (DAT) and the Sparse Channel Transformer (SCT). In MPBM, DAT and SCT alternate at a defined ratio, enabling the effective aggregation of contextual information from multiple perspectives of sparse correspondences and achieving complementary interactions between them. DAT is tailored to capture both local and global spatial contexts between correspondences while managing computational complexity through a simple clustering operation. In contrast, SCT introduces a top-k masking mechanism within channel-wise self-attention to prioritize key channel information, thereby improving the network’s ability to focus on the most influential features. Through the integration of these design elements, PTCNet demonstrates robust feature representation capabilities and offers state-of-the-art performance with efficient parameter usage, robustness, and generalization capabilities across different datasets and descriptors. Notably, our proposed PTCNet achieves a significant improvement in mAP5 ∘ over the second-best method on outdoor unknown scenes. The code is provided at https://github.com/mxyttkx/PTCNet . Published: 2026-03-27T03:43:01+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.774 (consider)
