In this paper, we introduce a novel pure Transformer-based two-view correspondence pruning network (called PTCNet). Unlike existing methods that rely on Multilayer Perceptrons, PTCNet leverages a Transformer backbone to achieve superior performance and higher efficiency. Specifically, we propose the Multi-Perspective Blending Module (MPBM), which integrates two distinct Transformer-based blocks: the Dual-Aggregation Transformer (DAT) and the Sparse Channel Transformer (SCT). In MPBM, DAT and SCT alternate at a defined ratio, enabling the effective aggregation of contextual information from multiple perspectives of sparse correspondences and achieving complementary interactions between them. DAT is tailored to capture both local and global spatial contexts between correspondences while managing computational complexity through a simple clustering operation. In contrast, SCT introduces a top-k masking mechanism within channel-wise self-attention to prioritize key channel information, thereby improving the network’s ability to focus on the most influential features. Through the integration of these design elements, PTCNet demonstrates robust feature representation capabilities and offers state-of-the-art performance with efficient parameter usage, robustness, and generalization capabilities across different datasets and descriptors. Notably, our proposed PTCNet achieves a significant improvement in mAP5 ∘ over the second-best method on outdoor unknown scenes. The code is provided at https://github.com/mxyttkx/PTCNet .

item.title: CroBIM-V: Memory-Quality Controlled Remote Sensing Referring Video Object Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Haochen Jiang
Yuzhe Sun
Zhe Dong
Tianzhu Liu
Yanfeng Gu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing video referring object segmentation (RS-RVOS) is challenged by weak target saliency and severe visual information truncation in dynamic scenes, making it extremely difficult to maintain discriminative target representations during segmentation. Moreover, progress in this field is hindered by the absence of large-scale dedicated benchmarks, while existing models are often affected by biased initial memory construction that impairs accurate instance localization in complex scenarios, as well as indiscriminate memory accumulation that encodes noise from occlusions or misclassifications, leading to persistent error propagation. This paper advances RS-RVOS research through dual contributions in data and methodology. First, we construct RS-RVOS Bench, the first large-scale benchmark comprising 111 video sequences, about 25,000 frames, and 213,000 temporal referring annotations. Unlike common RVOS benchmarks where many expressions are written with access to the full video context, our dataset adopts a strict causality-aware annotation strategy in which linguistic references are generated solely from the target state in the initial frame. Second, we propose a memory-quality-aware online referring segmentation framework, termed Memory Quality Control with Segment Anything Model (MQC-SAM). MQC-SAM introduces a temporal motion consistency module for initial memory calibration, leveraging short-term motion trajectory priors to correct structural deviations and establish accurate memory anchoring. Furthermore, it incorporates a decoupled attention-based memory integration mechanism with dynamic quality assessment, selectively updating high-confidence semantic features while filtering unreliable information, thereby effectively preventing error accumulation and propagation. Extensive experiments on RS-RVOS Bench demonstrate that MQC-SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance. Published: 2026-03-27T19:49:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.773 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haochen Jiang; Yuzhe Sun; Zhe Dong; Tianzhu Liu; Yanfeng Gu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3678310

Relevance: 0.773 (consider)

Remote sensing video referring object segmentation (RS-RVOS) is challenged by weak target saliency and severe visual information truncation in dynamic scenes, making it extremely difficult to maintain discriminative target representations during segmentation. Moreover, progress in this field is hindered by the absence of large-scale dedicated benchmarks, while existing models are often affected by biased initial memory construction that impairs accurate instance localization in complex scenarios, as well as indiscriminate memory accumulation that encodes noise from occlusions or misclassifications, leading to persistent error propagation. This paper advances RS-RVOS research through dual contributions in data and methodology. First, we construct RS-RVOS Bench, the first large-scale benchmark comprising 111 video sequences, about 25,000 frames, and 213,000 temporal referring annotations. Unlike common RVOS benchmarks where many expressions are written with access to the full video context, our dataset adopts a strict causality-aware annotation strategy in which linguistic references are generated solely from the target state in the initial frame. Second, we propose a memory-quality-aware online referring segmentation framework, termed Memory Quality Control with Segment Anything Model (MQC-SAM). MQC-SAM introduces a temporal motion consistency module for initial memory calibration, leveraging short-term motion trajectory priors to correct structural deviations and establish accurate memory anchoring. Furthermore, it incorporates a decoupled attention-based memory integration mechanism with dynamic quality assessment, selectively updating high-confidence semantic features while filtering unreliable information, thereby effectively preventing error accumulation and propagation. Extensive experiments on RS-RVOS Bench demonstrate that MQC-SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance.

item.title: Robust solution for the ambiguous blind perspective-n-line problem
item.dc:creator: Xiangxi Tian
Jinyuan Shao
Shuang Li
Jie Shan
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Estimating camera pose from lines is compelling in structured environments, yet it is difficult when 2D–3D line correspondences are unknown and inherently ambiguous. In the Blind Perspective-n-Line (BPnL) setting studied in prior work, the pose and correspondences must be solved jointly, without any correspondence priors. This difficult problem becomes more challenging due to fragmented detections, repeated and parallel structures, occlusions, and detector noise for the 2D lines in images. As a result, a single 3D scene line may correspond to multiple, or none 2D image lines, yielding 1-to-n correspondence with n ∈ { 0 , 1 , ⋯ } " role="presentation"> n ∈ { 0 , 1 , ⋯ } n ∈ { 0 , 1 , ⋯ } . We address BPnL under this ambiguity by combining imaging geometry with robust correspondence handling using two solvers: (1) the k-start solver performs iterative optimization with multi-start global search to avoid local optima under one-to-many and missing matches. (2) the BPnLNet, a neural network-based solver, estimates soft correspondence probabilities and incorporates them into a geometry-aware objective function, naturally supporting 1-to-n correspondence. Experiments on indoor and outdoor scenes show that both solvers substantially outperform a classical BPnL baseline by at least 60% in accuracy and achieve competitive quality with point-based methods, while remaining robust when correspondence is ambiguous or absent. Furthermore, this study demonstrates that the designed BPnLNet solver attains satisfactory inter-domain generalizability provided that the training data are sufficiently representative. These results highlight the contribution and practicality of lines for pose estimation when point features are unreliable or unavailable. The source code and documentation are available at https://github.com/OpenGSAM/bpnl-solver. Published: 2026-03-27T22:32:24+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.771 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiangxi Tian; Jinyuan Shao; Shuang Li; Jie Shan

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.011

Relevance: 0.771 (consider)

Estimating camera pose from lines is compelling in structured environments, yet it is difficult when 2D–3D line correspondences are unknown and inherently ambiguous. In the Blind Perspective-n-Line (BPnL) setting studied in prior work, the pose and correspondences must be solved jointly, without any correspondence priors. This difficult problem becomes more challenging due to fragmented detections, repeated and parallel structures, occlusions, and detector noise for the 2D lines in images. As a result, a single 3D scene line may correspond to multiple, or none 2D image lines, yielding 1-to-n correspondence with n ∈ { 0 , 1 , ⋯ } " role="presentation"> n ∈ { 0 , 1 , ⋯ } n ∈ { 0 , 1 , ⋯ } . We address BPnL under this ambiguity by combining imaging geometry with robust correspondence handling using two solvers: (1) the k-start solver performs iterative optimization with multi-start global search to avoid local optima under one-to-many and missing matches. (2) the BPnLNet, a neural network-based solver, estimates soft correspondence probabilities and incorporates them into a geometry-aware objective function, naturally supporting 1-to-n correspondence. Experiments on indoor and outdoor scenes show that both solvers substantially outperform a classical BPnL baseline by at least 60% in accuracy and achieve competitive quality with point-based methods, while remaining robust when correspondence is ambiguous or absent. Furthermore, this study demonstrates that the designed BPnLNet solver attains satisfactory inter-domain generalizability provided that the training data are sufficiently representative. These results highlight the contribution and practicality of lines for pose estimation when point features are unreliable or unavailable. The source code and documentation are available at https://github.com/OpenGSAM/bpnl-solver.

item.title: Model knowledge-prior embedded subspace learning network for hyperspectral image classification
item.dc:creator: Xiangyu Nie
Zhaohui Xue
Jun Li
item.description: Deep learning shows significant promise over representation models in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification, owing to its data-driven nature for automated feature learning. Meanwhile, compared with the black-box nature of deep learning, representation models offer greater theoretical guarantees and interpretability. To leverage the complementary advantages of both, we formulate the classification task by coupling model-driven with data-driven, and a model knowledge-prior embedded subspace learning network (MKPSL-Net) is presented. Firstly, the low-rank and sparse representation (LRSR) model incorporating deep spatial-spectral integrated dictionary learning (DS 2 IDL) is employed to establish significant subspace prior relationships, where DS 2 IDL learns dictionary atoms in a spatial-spectral self-supervised manner to enhance the subspace representation performance of the model. Secondly, to address the limited capability of LRSR in spatial structure perception and discriminative feature extraction, a contextual information aggregation module (CIAM) is designed to integrate multi-scale features across various receptive field ranges. Thirdly, the proposed subspace prior attention mechanism (SPAM) is utilized to dynamically capture the prior correlations among different subspace components, enhancing the discriminability of the network between inter-class differences. Finally, a composite residual-label joint loss (RLJL) is developed to guide learnable parameters optimization with both residual and label-driven objectives. Experimental results and analyses across four diverse scenarios show that the proposed network exhibits better classification performance than the state-of-the-art methods while exhibiting superior generalization ability. Published: 2026-03-28T00:36:16+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.770 (consider)

Authors: Xiangyu Nie; Zhaohui Xue; Jun Li

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113587

Relevance: 0.770 (consider)

Deep learning shows significant promise over representation models in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification, owing to its data-driven nature for automated feature learning. Meanwhile, compared with the black-box nature of deep learning, representation models offer greater theoretical guarantees and interpretability. To leverage the complementary advantages of both, we formulate the classification task by coupling model-driven with data-driven, and a model knowledge-prior embedded subspace learning network (MKPSL-Net) is presented. Firstly, the low-rank and sparse representation (LRSR) model incorporating deep spatial-spectral integrated dictionary learning (DS 2 IDL) is employed to establish significant subspace prior relationships, where DS 2 IDL learns dictionary atoms in a spatial-spectral self-supervised manner to enhance the subspace representation performance of the model. Secondly, to address the limited capability of LRSR in spatial structure perception and discriminative feature extraction, a contextual information aggregation module (CIAM) is designed to integrate multi-scale features across various receptive field ranges. Thirdly, the proposed subspace prior attention mechanism (SPAM) is utilized to dynamically capture the prior correlations among different subspace components, enhancing the discriminability of the network between inter-class differences. Finally, a composite residual-label joint loss (RLJL) is developed to guide learnable parameters optimization with both residual and label-driven objectives. Experimental results and analyses across four diverse scenarios show that the proposed network exhibits better classification performance than the state-of-the-art methods while exhibiting superior generalization ability.

item.title: STFO-Diff: A State Feedback Optimization-Based Diffusion Framework for Multimodal Medical Image Fusion
item.dc:creator: Yu Cheng
Han Zhang
Yufang Dong
Mingjing Han
Xiongwen Quan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: Diffusion models demonstrate strong potential for improving image quality and preserving fine-grained details in medical image fusion. However, the existing diffusion-based fusion methods lack modality-specific feature estimation during the iterative denoising process, resulting in the gradual entanglement of features. We propose a state-feedback optimization-based diffusion (STFO-Diff) framework, which can extract the generative state and enable accurate feedback learning, thereby achieving fine-grained modeling over modality-specific image generation. Specifically, we design a state measurement module (SMM) comprising two key components: a sparse basis decomposer (SBD) and a modality perception decomposer (MPD). The SBD provides a quantifiable physical representation of the fusion state by decomposing the intermediate fused image into distinct components. In parallel, the MPD provides a modality-aware evaluation by estimating the preservation and completeness of modality-specific information. In STFO-Diff, the estimated states are leveraged to optimize and supervise the reverse diffusion denoising process, where a state feedback mechanism adaptively maintains the modality integrity of modality information in the fused image. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, demonstrating its superior performance in medical image fusion tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/zhanglabNKU/STFO-DIff Published: 2026-03-27T19:51:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.770 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Cheng; Han Zhang; Yufang Dong; Mingjing Han; Xiongwen Quan

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tnnls.2026.3677123

Relevance: 0.770 (consider)

Diffusion models demonstrate strong potential for improving image quality and preserving fine-grained details in medical image fusion. However, the existing diffusion-based fusion methods lack modality-specific feature estimation during the iterative denoising process, resulting in the gradual entanglement of features. We propose a state-feedback optimization-based diffusion (STFO-Diff) framework, which can extract the generative state and enable accurate feedback learning, thereby achieving fine-grained modeling over modality-specific image generation. Specifically, we design a state measurement module (SMM) comprising two key components: a sparse basis decomposer (SBD) and a modality perception decomposer (MPD). The SBD provides a quantifiable physical representation of the fusion state by decomposing the intermediate fused image into distinct components. In parallel, the MPD provides a modality-aware evaluation by estimating the preservation and completeness of modality-specific information. In STFO-Diff, the estimated states are leveraged to optimize and supervise the reverse diffusion denoising process, where a state feedback mechanism adaptively maintains the modality integrity of modality information in the fused image. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, demonstrating its superior performance in medical image fusion tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/zhanglabNKU/STFO-DIff

item.title: ORDiffSR: An effective one-step diffusion network for single remote sensing image super-resolution
item.dc:creator: Ting Tang
Jiangping Liu
Xiaoling Luo
Xiaojing Gao
Xueliang Fu
Xin Pan
item.description: Recently, there has been considerable advancement in image generation techniques that utilize diffusion models. Pre-trained models like Stable Diffusion leverage strong natural image priors to enhance reconstructed image quality, but their training on natural images creates a data distribution gap with remote sensing images (RSIs), hindering direct transfer. Moreover, current methods for super-resolution in remote sensing that utilize diffusion techniques initiate from random noise and rely on multi-step iterations, leading to high computational costs and noise-induced uncertainty that impairs precise detail recovery. To address these issues, this paper proposes ORDiffSR, an efficient one-step denoising diffusion method for single RSI super-resolution. Using SDXL as the generative prior, it takes low-resolution RSI directly as the diffusion starting point to reduce noise-related uncertainty. To achieve efficient adaptation to complex ground objects and multi-scale features in RSIs, we propose an efficient VAE module and a one-step denoising U-Net network, and adopt LoRA to fine-tune the one-step denoising U-Net network. Furthermore, to maintain the visual fidelity and distribution consistency of the generated outputs, we incorporate a Variational Score Distillation (VSD) loss into the latent space, thereby promoting strong distributional alignment between generated images and authentic high-resolution images. Extensive experiments were conducted on four public RSI datasets, AID, DOTA, DIOR, and WHU-RS19. The results demonstrate that ORDiffSR can accomplish super-resolution reconstruction with a single diffusion step, achieving an inference speed that is over 150 times faster than the EDiffSR method. Meanwhile, it significantly outperforms existing CNN, Transformer, GAN, and diffusion model approaches in terms of both subjective and objective evaluations, providing a novel solution for efficient super-resolution of RSIs. The code is available at https://github.com/TeresaTing/ORDiffSR . Published: 2026-03-28T16:12:59+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.770 (consider)

Authors: Ting Tang; Jiangping Liu; Xiaoling Luo; Xiaojing Gao; Xueliang Fu; Xin Pan

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132254

Relevance: 0.770 (consider)

Recently, there has been considerable advancement in image generation techniques that utilize diffusion models. Pre-trained models like Stable Diffusion leverage strong natural image priors to enhance reconstructed image quality, but their training on natural images creates a data distribution gap with remote sensing images (RSIs), hindering direct transfer. Moreover, current methods for super-resolution in remote sensing that utilize diffusion techniques initiate from random noise and rely on multi-step iterations, leading to high computational costs and noise-induced uncertainty that impairs precise detail recovery. To address these issues, this paper proposes ORDiffSR, an efficient one-step denoising diffusion method for single RSI super-resolution. Using SDXL as the generative prior, it takes low-resolution RSI directly as the diffusion starting point to reduce noise-related uncertainty. To achieve efficient adaptation to complex ground objects and multi-scale features in RSIs, we propose an efficient VAE module and a one-step denoising U-Net network, and adopt LoRA to fine-tune the one-step denoising U-Net network. Furthermore, to maintain the visual fidelity and distribution consistency of the generated outputs, we incorporate a Variational Score Distillation (VSD) loss into the latent space, thereby promoting strong distributional alignment between generated images and authentic high-resolution images. Extensive experiments were conducted on four public RSI datasets, AID, DOTA, DIOR, and WHU-RS19. The results demonstrate that ORDiffSR can accomplish super-resolution reconstruction with a single diffusion step, achieving an inference speed that is over 150 times faster than the EDiffSR method. Meanwhile, it significantly outperforms existing CNN, Transformer, GAN, and diffusion model approaches in terms of both subjective and objective evaluations, providing a novel solution for efficient super-resolution of RSIs. The code is available at https://github.com/TeresaTing/ORDiffSR .

item.title: SD-Net: A Synergistic Dual-Branch Network for Fine-Grained Building Extraction
item.dc:creator: Weidong Tao
Yanting Li
Zunfang Liu
Yidan Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Building footprint extraction from high-resolution remote sensing images (RSIs) is essential for applications such as urban planning, disaster assessment, and infrastructure management. Existing methods often struggle to maintain semantic consistency while precisely delineating boundaries due to complex backgrounds and variations in building scale and morphology, leading to fractured contours, blurred edges, or mis-segmentation. To address these challenges, a Synergistic Dual-Branch Network (SD-Net) is proposed, which jointly models semantic and boundary information via a shared encoder with separate semantic and boundary decoders constrained by a joint loss, with gradient detachment to stabilize multi-task optimization.A multi-scale interaction module, the Interactive Dual-LKA Block (ID-LKA), enhances context modeling for buildings of varying scales. Furthermore, a lightweight Attention Refinement Cascade Module (ARCM) refines high-level features by adaptively selecting discriminative channel and spatial information. Experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate that SD-Net outperforms mainstream segmentation models in mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) and F1-score (F1) and achieves more stable boundary integrity. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each module, confirming the method's effectiveness and efficiency for resource-constrained automatic building extraction tasks. Published: 2026-03-27T19:50:21+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.768 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Weidong Tao; Yanting Li; Zunfang Liu; Yidan Zhao

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3678319

Relevance: 0.768 (consider)

Building footprint extraction from high-resolution remote sensing images (RSIs) is essential for applications such as urban planning, disaster assessment, and infrastructure management. Existing methods often struggle to maintain semantic consistency while precisely delineating boundaries due to complex backgrounds and variations in building scale and morphology, leading to fractured contours, blurred edges, or mis-segmentation. To address these challenges, a Synergistic Dual-Branch Network (SD-Net) is proposed, which jointly models semantic and boundary information via a shared encoder with separate semantic and boundary decoders constrained by a joint loss, with gradient detachment to stabilize multi-task optimization.A multi-scale interaction module, the Interactive Dual-LKA Block (ID-LKA), enhances context modeling for buildings of varying scales. Furthermore, a lightweight Attention Refinement Cascade Module (ARCM) refines high-level features by adaptively selecting discriminative channel and spatial information. Experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate that SD-Net outperforms mainstream segmentation models in mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) and F1-score (F1) and achieves more stable boundary integrity. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each module, confirming the method's effectiveness and efficiency for resource-constrained automatic building extraction tasks.

item.title: Wavelet-enhanced transformers for real-time event-based object detection in low-light environments
item.dc:creator: Yangjie Cui
Zhan Tu
Daochun Li
Boyang Gao
Yiwei Zhang
Jinwu Xiang
Xin Dong
item.description: Neuromorphic event cameras exhibit superior performance in low-light environments owing to their high dynamic range and microsecond-level temporal resolution. However, the intensive background activity (BA) noise inherent in low-illumination event streams significantly compromises detection accuracy. Conventional strategies predominantly utilize a decoupled pipeline where denoising and detection are treated as independent stages, often resulting in irreversible information loss and suboptimal performance due to mismatched optimization objectives. To address these challenges, we propose an end-to-end Wavelet-Enhanced Transformer detection network (WE-DETR). The proposed framework integrates the wavelet transform directly into the feature extraction hierarchy to adaptively suppress stochastic noise and enhance multi-scale structural representations from event accumulations. This is followed by a Transformer-based detection head designed to model global spatiotemporal dependencies for precise object localization and recognition. Extensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets–DSEC, Gen1, and 1Mpx–demonstrate that WE-DETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, hardware deployment on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX achieves real-time inference at approximately 35 FPS using TensorRT FP16, validating the framework’s suitability for resource-constrained embedded platforms. Field experiments in diverse outdoor scenarios under illumination as low as 2.7 lux further confirm the robustness and practical utility of WE-DETR in extremely low-light environments. Published: 2026-03-28T07:29:57+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.768 (consider)

Neuromorphic event cameras exhibit superior performance in low-light environments owing to their high dynamic range and microsecond-level temporal resolution. However, the intensive background activity (BA) noise inherent in low-illumination event streams significantly compromises detection accuracy. Conventional strategies predominantly utilize a decoupled pipeline where denoising and detection are treated as independent stages, often resulting in irreversible information loss and suboptimal performance due to mismatched optimization objectives. To address these challenges, we propose an end-to-end Wavelet-Enhanced Transformer detection network (WE-DETR). The proposed framework integrates the wavelet transform directly into the feature extraction hierarchy to adaptively suppress stochastic noise and enhance multi-scale structural representations from event accumulations. This is followed by a Transformer-based detection head designed to model global spatiotemporal dependencies for precise object localization and recognition. Extensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets–DSEC, Gen1, and 1Mpx–demonstrate that WE-DETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, hardware deployment on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX achieves real-time inference at approximately 35 FPS using TensorRT FP16, validating the framework’s suitability for resource-constrained embedded platforms. Field experiments in diverse outdoor scenarios under illumination as low as 2.7 lux further confirm the robustness and practical utility of WE-DETR in extremely low-light environments.

item.title: High-frequency geometry enhanced graph attention network for hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion
item.dc:creator: Ziqing Ma
Chang Yang
Zhichang Guo
Yao Li
item.description: Fusing low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) is essential for hyperspectral image super-resolution, enabling the generation of high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI) crucial for detailed analysis. Although deep learning methods have advanced this field, many existing approaches focus primarily on deep spatial feature extraction for the recovery of homogeneous regions, often overlooking the explicit modeling of high-frequency geometric features such as edges, corners, curvatures, and other detailed textures. This oversight results in blurred object contours, loss of fine structural details, and spectral distortion, which severely hinders precise analysis in critical HSI applications. To address these limitations, we propose a high-frequency geometry-enhanced graph attention network (GGAT) for hyperspectral and multispectral image (HS-MS) fusion. GGAT effectively restores high-frequency geometric structures in LR-HSI by leveraging nonlocal self-similarity and explicitly modeling high-frequency geometric features. Our approach utilizes image gradients and higher-order derivatives to capture and restore these structures, formulating the task as the joint recovery of the LR-HSI and its spatial derivative images. We decompose LR-HSI and its gradient representations into patches, each forming a graph node. Edges connect query nodes to neighbors, with adaptive weights learned via a graph attention mechanism from HR-MSI to assess neighbor importance. The query node is updated by aggregating these weighted features to explicitly reconstruct high-frequency geometric structures, thereby obtaining HR-HSI with sharp contours, clear detailed structures, and improved spectral fidelity. Experimental results on both simulated and real datasets demonstrate that GGAT significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art techniques in HS-MS fusion, both quantitatively and qualitatively. Published: 2026-03-27T16:49:24+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.768 (consider)

Journal: Pattern Recognition

Relevance: 0.768 (consider)

Fusing low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) is essential for hyperspectral image super-resolution, enabling the generation of high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI) crucial for detailed analysis. Although deep learning methods have advanced this field, many existing approaches focus primarily on deep spatial feature extraction for the recovery of homogeneous regions, often overlooking the explicit modeling of high-frequency geometric features such as edges, corners, curvatures, and other detailed textures. This oversight results in blurred object contours, loss of fine structural details, and spectral distortion, which severely hinders precise analysis in critical HSI applications. To address these limitations, we propose a high-frequency geometry-enhanced graph attention network (GGAT) for hyperspectral and multispectral image (HS-MS) fusion. GGAT effectively restores high-frequency geometric structures in LR-HSI by leveraging nonlocal self-similarity and explicitly modeling high-frequency geometric features. Our approach utilizes image gradients and higher-order derivatives to capture and restore these structures, formulating the task as the joint recovery of the LR-HSI and its spatial derivative images. We decompose LR-HSI and its gradient representations into patches, each forming a graph node. Edges connect query nodes to neighbors, with adaptive weights learned via a graph attention mechanism from HR-MSI to assess neighbor importance. The query node is updated by aggregating these weighted features to explicitly reconstruct high-frequency geometric structures, thereby obtaining HR-HSI with sharp contours, clear detailed structures, and improved spectral fidelity. Experimental results on both simulated and real datasets demonstrate that GGAT significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art techniques in HS-MS fusion, both quantitatively and qualitatively.

item.title: Simple-RF: Regularizing Sparse Input Radiance Fields with Simpler Solutions
item.dc:creator: Nagabhushan Somraj
Sai Harsha Mupparaju
Adithyan Karanayil
Rajiv Soundararajan
item.description: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) show impressive performances in photo-realistic free-view rendering of scenes. Recent improvements such as TensoRF and ZipNeRF employ explicit models for faster optimization and rendering. However, all these radiance fields require a dense sampling of images in the given scene for effective training. Their performances degrade significantly when only a sparse set of views is available. Existing depth priors used to supervise the radiance fields are either sparse or suffer from generalization issues. We seek to learn scene-specific dense depth priors to regularize the radiance fields. Further, we desire a framework of regularizations that can work across different radiance field models. We observe that certain features of the radiance fields, such as positional encoding, number of decomposed tensor components or size of the hash table, cause overfitting in the sparse-input scenario. We design augmented models by reducing the capacity of these features and train them along with the main radiance field. These augmented models learn simpler solutions, which estimate better depth in certain regions. By supervising the main radiance field with such depths, we significantly improve the performance of the radiance fields on popular forward-facing and 360° datasets by employing the above regularization. Published: 2026-03-28T20:34:58+00:00 Venue: ACM Transactions on Graphics Score: 0.765 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nagabhushan Somraj; Sai Harsha Mupparaju; Adithyan Karanayil; Rajiv Soundararajan

Journal: ACM Transactions on Graphics

DOI: 10.1145/3806041

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) show impressive performances in photo-realistic free-view rendering of scenes. Recent improvements such as TensoRF and ZipNeRF employ explicit models for faster optimization and rendering. However, all these radiance fields require a dense sampling of images in the given scene for effective training. Their performances degrade significantly when only a sparse set of views is available. Existing depth priors used to supervise the radiance fields are either sparse or suffer from generalization issues. We seek to learn scene-specific dense depth priors to regularize the radiance fields. Further, we desire a framework of regularizations that can work across different radiance field models. We observe that certain features of the radiance fields, such as positional encoding, number of decomposed tensor components or size of the hash table, cause overfitting in the sparse-input scenario. We design augmented models by reducing the capacity of these features and train them along with the main radiance field. These augmented models learn simpler solutions, which estimate better depth in certain regions. By supervising the main radiance field with such depths, we significantly improve the performance of the radiance fields on popular forward-facing and 360° datasets by employing the above regularization.

item.title: HAQ-ViT: A Hardware-Aware Post-Training Quantization for Efficient Vision Transformer Inference
item.dc:creator: Shiqi Zhao
Zhengjie Wang
Haozu Sun
Sibo Wang
Zihan Chen
Changzeng Fu
Jie Yang
Mohamad Sawan
item.description: In recent years, Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated outstanding performance in the field of computer vision. However, the quadratic complexity of their self-attention mechanism results in a significant increase in model storage requirements and computational overhead, which has become a critical bottleneck hindering their widespread adoption. Consequently, compressing ViTs has emerged as a major area of research focus, with ViTs quantization being a prominent direction within this domain. This paper systematically analyzes and identifies three key optimization opportunities in existing INT8 quantized ViTs models: 1) The numerical distribution of the query-key vectors in the attention matrix exhibits a 40.66% occurrence of consecutive identical values spanning 3 bits; 2) The activation sparsity across different network layers displays significant heterogeneity; and 3) Shallow features can already provide reliable classification confidence. Based on these findings, we propose HAQ-ViT, a hardware aware post-training quantization acceleration framework. First, we introduce a hybrid quantization scheme grounded in the improved Booth algorithm. By designing a hardware-friendly bit sequence skipping strategy, we enable the omission of multiplication operations involving consecutive identical bit patterns, building upon standard 8-bit quantization. Secondly, we propose a dynamic layer sparsification strategy. By analyzing the inter-layer differences in activation distribution, we construct an adaptive threshold selection function, thereby reducing the sparsification decision complexity from O ( n 2 ) to O ( kn 2 ), where k ∈ (0, 1) is a predefined scaling factor. Finally, we introduce a global quantization adaptive early exit mechanism, where multiple lightweight classification heads are strategically inserted to monitor confidence entropy in real-time. When the entropy exceeds a defined threshold, the computations are terminated early. Experimental results demonstrate that the HAQ-ViT framework, evaluated on the ImageNet dataset, maintains inference accuracy at a sparsity rate of 76% (with a 0.068% improvement over the baseline model). With the integration of early exit heads, the accuracy experiences a marginal decrease of only 0.004%, and at a sparsity rate of 94%, the energy consumption for a single inference is significantly reduced from 64.6 mJ to 10.91 mJ. Published: 2026-03-27T16:18:53+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.765 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shiqi Zhao; Zhengjie Wang; Haozu Sun; Sibo Wang; Zihan Chen; Changzeng Fu; Jie Yang; Mohamad Sawan

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115830

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

In recent years, Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated outstanding performance in the field of computer vision. However, the quadratic complexity of their self-attention mechanism results in a significant increase in model storage requirements and computational overhead, which has become a critical bottleneck hindering their widespread adoption. Consequently, compressing ViTs has emerged as a major area of research focus, with ViTs quantization being a prominent direction within this domain. This paper systematically analyzes and identifies three key optimization opportunities in existing INT8 quantized ViTs models: 1) The numerical distribution of the query-key vectors in the attention matrix exhibits a 40.66% occurrence of consecutive identical values spanning 3 bits; 2) The activation sparsity across different network layers displays significant heterogeneity; and 3) Shallow features can already provide reliable classification confidence. Based on these findings, we propose HAQ-ViT, a hardware aware post-training quantization acceleration framework. First, we introduce a hybrid quantization scheme grounded in the improved Booth algorithm. By designing a hardware-friendly bit sequence skipping strategy, we enable the omission of multiplication operations involving consecutive identical bit patterns, building upon standard 8-bit quantization. Secondly, we propose a dynamic layer sparsification strategy. By analyzing the inter-layer differences in activation distribution, we construct an adaptive threshold selection function, thereby reducing the sparsification decision complexity from O ( n 2 ) to O ( kn 2 ), where k ∈ (0, 1) is a predefined scaling factor. Finally, we introduce a global quantization adaptive early exit mechanism, where multiple lightweight classification heads are strategically inserted to monitor confidence entropy in real-time. When the entropy exceeds a defined threshold, the computations are terminated early. Experimental results demonstrate that the HAQ-ViT framework, evaluated on the ImageNet dataset, maintains inference accuracy at a sparsity rate of 76% (with a 0.068% improvement over the baseline model). With the integration of early exit heads, the accuracy experiences a marginal decrease of only 0.004%, and at a sparsity rate of 94%, the energy consumption for a single inference is significantly reduced from 64.6 mJ to 10.91 mJ.
